source: Krumhansl & Kessler (1982) probe-tone profiles, as tabulated in Krumhansl, Cognitive Foundations of Musical Pitch (1990)

Temperley-Kostka-Payne (CBMS) key profiles: Temperley, Music and Probability (MIT Press, 2007), derived from the Kostka-Payne theory-textbook corpus. Vectors verified digit-for-digit against music21 analysis/discrete.py (TemperleyKostkaPayneKeyWeights) and Sapp's Humdrum keycor. Documented biases (use as an OPT-IN alternative, validate on the target corpus): well-balanced for MAJOR keys (corrects the Krumhansl-Kessler dominant bias); but a known tendency to read the RELATIVE MAJOR as tonic in minor keys. Added A6 brief-9/research-9: on the SWD residual it recovers the dominant-substitution (e.g. A maj read as E maj) and parallel near-tie cases under the engine's Pearson scoring, trading kk-1982.1's dominant bias for a relative-major bias — hence opt-in pending full-corpus regression scoring, never a silent default change (the infer_key default is the A5/A7 stability contract).
